data.title: Eldorado Gold: Strong Q2 Performance Driven By Record Gold Prices, Skouries Development On Track (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The gold mining industry is experiencing its strongest fundamental environment in decades. Gold prices have risen 40% year-over-year (Y/Y), at times surpassing the $3,500 per ounce (p/oz) level in the second quarter of 2025. This was driven by a combination of inflationary pressures, geopolitical tensions, and a strong buying rally by central banks. Meanwhile, precious metal miners, including the GDX Gold Mining ETF, have generated returns of over 70% in 2025.

This represents a fundamental shift for the gold market in recent times. Unlike previous bull runs for the metal, driven primarily by increased demand stemming from the crisis, the current cycle reflects sustained institutional positioning amid uncertainty over global monetary and trade policy. The World Gold Council reported a record Q2 2025 demand of $132B (+45% Y/Y), driven by ETF inflows, which accelerated at their fastest pace since 2020 in the first half of the year, and persistent central bank buying, which added another round 166 tonnes to its reserves.

For mid-tier producers like Eldorado Gold (NYSE:EGO), this is music to their ears. With average total operating costs hovering around $1,500 p/oz, compared to gold prices near $3,300, profit margins have expanded to historic levels, positively impacting the cash flow needed to continue funding important projects and return capital to shareholders.

Q2 2025 operating results

Diving into the numbers for this past quarter, Eldorado’s Q2 2025 production of 133,769 ounces reported its highest Q2 performance since 2020. The company’s performance was also significantly ahead of expectations due to a drawdown at Kisladag and improved production at Lamaque following increased processing of the second bulk sample from the Ormaque pit, partially offset by a lower concentrate grade per tonne which fell to 6.62 g/t from 6.95 g/t in Q2 2024 at the Canadian operation.

The increase in production was also due to seasonal dynamics, with the first quarter generally affected by winter conditions at Kisladag and their impact on longer leaching cycles. This was reflected in a drop of more than 25% ounces produced during Q1 2025, the company's lowest production in nearly two years. Despite this, and following a solid performance in Q2, company management decided to maintain fairly conservative guidance for the full year, ranging from 460,000 to 500,000 ounces, representing a 4-12% decrease compared to 2024.

Looking at the performance of each project, we see diverging trends worth highlighting. Kisladag delivered strong operating performance with 46,058 ounces (+18% Y/Y) last quarter, benefiting from accelerating inventory drawdown, while the mine operation celebrated a significant milestone of 4Moz, taking Eldorado’s cumulative gold production in Turkey to over 5Moz. On the other hand, Lamaque (again) emerged as the most productive operation of the quarter with 50,640 ounces (+6.8% Y/Y) and lower total costs of $1,231 p/oz (-11% Y/Y), demonstrating strong operating efficiency driven by improved gold concentration grades per tonne and a better than expected bulk sample from the Ormaque deposit.

Olympias showed considerable improvement, with a 35% increase in gold production and a 30% decrease in total costs compared to the previous quarter, consolidating a recovery following the resolution of its labor disputes. Meanwhile, Efemcukuru was the weakest point in the production chain, with a drop to 21,093 ounces (-6% Y/Y), attributed to a lower gold concentration per tonne, which fell from 5.92 grams in Q2 2024 to 5.75 grams.

Finally, at the consolidated level, costs continued to show persistent upward pressure, with cash costs increasing to $1,064 per ounce (+13% Y/Y) compared to $940 a year ago, while AISCs followed a similar trajectory, rising 14% from $1,331 p/oz to $1,520 p/oz over the same period. However, this did not prevent margins from resuming their upward trend during the last quarter, driven by the sharp rise in gold prices.

Financial results

Financially, results were mixed, with adjusted earnings per share of $0.44 below the consensus of $0.46, although revenue of $452M (+52% Y/Y) easily beat the analysts’ consensus of $413M. Meanwhile, the company’s free cash flow generation continues to improve. Last quarter free cash flow (excluding the Skouries investment) of $61.5M almost doubled the $34M mark reported in Q1 2025 due to a solid operating cash flow of $158M (+41% Y/Y) and a great ability to continue capitalizing on the gold price momentum. As Skouries gets underway next year, we expect this FCF trend to consolidate, as the company will be able to free up a quantity of resources previously allocated to the development of the project, whether for share buybacks or to continue expanding through new acquisitions.

In fact, this cash flow allowed the company to carry out share repurchases during Q2 2025 for approximately $45M and strengthen its balance sheet, with record total liquidity above $1,000M, giving it greater financial flexibility.

Skouries: The Catalyst for Transformation

Total capital expenditures in the second quarter of 2025 totalized around $241M, with the majority of that being allocated to the Skouries project. This copper-gold project represents Eldorado’s largest strategic initiative, with construction now 70% complete and initial production expected in the first quarter of 2026 and commercial production beginning mid-next year. The project received a substantial investment of $117 million in the Q2 2025 to an accumulated of $706M since restart of construction.

The Skouries project's anticipated copper production, in addition to gold, represents a significant advantage given this material's role in the energy transition, which gives Eldorado an attractive advantage in the coming years. If the project schedule progresses as planned, the company is positioned for significant production growth by 2027. Estimates for Skouries include proven and probable reserves of 3.7Moz of gold and 1.7 billion pounds of copper, with expected annual production of roughly 140,000 ounces of gold and 67 million pounds of copper over a 20-year mine life, which will be added to Eldorado's portfolio in the coming years.

Valuation and the path toward $2.5B in gold sales

Eldorado's stock has performed exceptionally well this year, rising more than 50% on the back of gold's momentum and Skouries' expectations. While the company lagged its industry peers somewhat in the early months of 2025, the longer-term picture tells a different story.

In terms of valuation, we see that EGO remains well below the industry average according to various multiple criteria, still indicating room for further growth despite the strong appreciation we've seen so far this year.

Following a sum-of-parts approach, and valuing current operations at the industry average of 8.8 times a normalized EBITDA of $450M, we obtain an EV of $4B. Adding Skouries' NPV estimates of approximately $1.6B (with a gold price of $1,800 and copper at $3.8), the total enterprise value would amount to $5.6B or ~$27 per share, compared to a current EV of around $4.64B or ~$22 per share, representing a potential upside of 23% and assuming fairly conservative criteria when valuing the Skouries project. Similarly, with a P/E of 14 and a trailing 12-month EPS of $2, we obtain a value per share of approximately $28, indicating a potential undervaluation of 24%, also very much in line with the previous criteria.

Furthermore, the cash flow projections from increased production resulting from the commissioning of Skouries raise the possibility that the company could soon reach $2.4 billion in gold sales revenue. The usual wear and tear at older mines would be offset in the coming years by the ramp-up of Skouries, making it the company's flagship project by 2030.

With the exception of Olympias, which recently experienced operational difficulties, we believe that following the recent plant expansion and operational improvements, production will improve to 85,000 ounces per year. Meanwhile, the remaining projects will see a gradual decline in performance over the coming years, having passed their peak production. In the case of Lamaque, the model assumes a production plateau between 180,000 and 185,000 ounces, with a brief boost from the development of Ormaque during 2026, before beginning a gradual decline to 170,000 ounces.

These projections take into account gold prices that are quite conservative in relation to some analysts' forecasts, as well as production aligned with the management goals set for the next couple of years. Assuming that the entire production output is placed on the market, with just over 660,000 ounces of gold and an estimated price of $3,800, Eldorado's revenues would exceed $2.5B by 2029. Meanwhile, including revenues from copper, revenues would reach almost $3B in 2030. This does not include other types of revenues, such as those from silver and zinc production, which have a very low weight in the company's earnings mix.

Adjusting these flows to determine the cash flow to the firm (FCFF) and discounting them at a WACC of 7% would yield an approximate value of $31 for EGO shares, indicating a potential undervaluation of 38% compared to current levels. If we also consider a possible decrease in the number of shares in the coming years as a result of the company's buybacks, this price could be even higher, while the discount rate criterion is also somewhat conservative given that the long-term WACC for the mining industry typically ranges between 5% and 6%.

On the other hand, performing a sensitivity analysis, we find that the risk-reward ratio remains attractive at current levels. Even under scenarios involving a decline in production, coupled with a contraction in gold prices, we note that the floor would be ~5% below current levels, at $21.50 per share, while the upside potential is considerable in the remaining scenarios.

Risks

As mentioned in a previous article, Eldorado operates through multiple jurisdictions with different risk profiles, which is one of the company's biggest risk factors. The resolution of labor disputes at Olympias and strong local support for the Skouries project helped stabilize operations in Greece. However, in Turkey, where two major EGO projects operate, regulatory and foreign exchange challenges continue to unfold. I strongly believe this deserves the utmost attention from investors given the rising cost inflation across all of EGO's operations in that country, which has significantly deteriorated its cost position relative to the industry.

On the opposite, Canadian operations, with a lower jurisdictional risk profile, coupled with the strategic value of assets like Skouries, present an opportunity for significant differentiation in the coming years and to significantly improve the company's overall cost structure. By spreading fixed costs across larger production volumes and benefiting from high-demand byproducts such as copper, Skouries should significantly reduce costs, addressing the current biggest problem and making it more competitive. However, I believe we won't see this until 2027, when the project is fully operational.

Conclusion

Eldorado represents an attractive investment opportunity at current levels. The completion and commissioning of Skouries is a game changer for the company to transform into a producer of over 600k ounces with significant free cash flow generation capacity. This, coupled with favorable fundamentals in the gold market positions the company for sustained growth in coming years. For these reasons I upgrade my target price estimate to a range between $28 and $31 considering conservative assumptions.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower-quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms — also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. On the positive side, the fund benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight in emerging-market bonds.

Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: Tonix Secures Fibromyalgia Approval - What Investors Should Know
data.description:
Investment Overview

It's stock may have been down nearly 15% today, but post-market, Tonix Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:TNXP) shares are up nearly 18% (according to TradingView) after the Chatham, New Jersey based pharma company announced the Food and Drug Agency ("FDA") approval of its fibromyalgia sublingual therapy (cyclobenzaprine HCl sublingual tablets) Tonmya.

I gave Tonix stock a Hold rating in my last post on the company, shared with Seeking Alpha readers in May, suggesting that investing in Tonix ahead of the the PDUFA ("Prescription Drug User Fee Act") approval date for Tonmya was a "risky bet", but concluded that "I lean bullish short-term if the FDA approves TNX-102, but would sell quickly post-approval".

Is that still my favoured strategy now that Tonmya has been approved?

Tonix Overview & Tonmya Approval Impact

According to a press release issued by Tonix earlier today:

Tonmya is a first-in-class, nonopioid, once-daily bedtime analgesic with a unique sublingual (under the tongue) formulation that is designed for rapid absorption into the bloodstream. Tonmya is the first new FDA approved therapy for the treatment of fibromyalgia in over 15 years.

The approval incorporated efficacy from two double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, Phase 3 clinical trials of nearly 1,000 patients in total that evaluated Tonmya as a bedtime treatment for fibromyalgia.

Across both Phase 3 trials, Tonmya significantly reduced daily pain scores compared to placebo at 14 weeks, the primary endpoint. Additionally, a greater percentage of study participants taking Tonmya experienced a clinically meaningful (≥30%) improvement in their pain after three months, compared to placebo.

Tonmya is expected to be available for adult patients in the U.S. with fibromyalgia beginning in the fourth quarter of this year.

Fibromyalgia is a chronic pain disorder that is understood to result from amplified sensory and pain signaling within the central nervous system. Fibromyalgia afflicts an estimated 10 million adults in the U.S., approximately 80% of whom are women.

Tonix reported its Q2 earnings on Monday 11th August, revealing net product revenues of ~$2m, down from $2.2m in the prior year quarter, which "reflected combined net sales of Zembrace, SymTouch, and Tosymra". According to the company's Q2 10Q filing:

Tonix Medicines, our commercial subsidiary, markets Zembrace® SymTouch® (sumatriptan injection) 3 mg and Tosymra® (sumatriptan nasal spray) 10 mg for the treatment of acute migraine with or without aura in adults.

Tonix spent $10.8m on R&D in Q2, and $16.2m on SG&A - up from $7.5m in the prior year quarter, owing to "spend on sales and marketing to progress pre-launch activities related to the potential FDA approval of TNX-102 SL for fibromyalgia".

Net loss for the quarter came to $(28.3m), or $(3.86) per share. Cash position was reported as $125.3m, and accumulated deficit $(775.8m). Besides three pre-clinal stage assets indicated for gastric cancer, Mpox or smallpox protection, and protection against viral pathogens for Warfighters, Tonix' pipeline consists of a Phase 1 stage anti-CD40 monoclonal antibody indicated for organ transplant rejections / autoimmune conditions.

In summary, Tonmya is a key asset for Tonix, whose shares currently trade at $60 (post-market), and whose market cap valuation stands at $430m (at the time of writing). Tonix stock has struggled to maintain compliance with Nasdaq listing rules in the past, resulting in, as I wrote in May:

the company completing a 1-for-32 reverse stock split in May 2022, then a 1-for-6.25 reverse stock split in May 2023, a 1-32 split in June last year, and remarkably, a 1-100 split in February this year.

I also added that:

No new fibromyalgia drugs have been approved in over 15 years - currently approved meds include Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) Cymbalta, and its generics, Viatris Inc.'s (VTRS) Lyrica, and its generics, Savella (milnacipran), marketed by AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), and cyclobenzaprine, the underlying ingredient in TNX-102, sold as Flexeril since 1977 for treatment of muscle spasms, and its generics.

According to Reuters, analysts have speculated that the drug could earn $800m in peak revenues, seven to eight years post launch. Cymbalta, which has lost patent protection, earned ~$200m in revenues last year in anxiety depression and pain markets, while Lyrica - marketed and sold by Viatris (VTRS) in similar indications - earned $128m in Q2 2025.

The key benefits of Tonmya, which as mentioned is available as a generic drug albeit not in a sublingual formulation, are summarised as follows in the following slide from a recent Tonix investor presentation:

What's The Post Approval Outlook For Tonmya

Tonix has recorded a >$(100m) net loss in each of the past three years, so with its current cash position of $125m potentially only lasting for 12 months - or less, if Tonix spends heavily on a commercial launch - I'd expect management to attempt to raise further funds via a (dilutive for existing investors) share price offering - perhaps as much as $100m.

The novel method of administration and lack of new therapies to treat fibromyalgia broadly plays in the company's favour, in my opinion, with Tonix' own research suggesting there is an appetite amongst patients and physicians to receive / prescribe a new therapy.

On the negative side, the drug has a slightly troubling safety profile, as evidenced by the below table summarising results from its RESILIENT study:

As we can see, instances of oral cavity adverse events were quite high in comparison to placebo - it would be problematic for the company if patients fail to be convinced by this tweak in administration of an already used drug.

In summary, while there are some strong positives to consider, with limited financial resources and in a market unused to change, Tonix may struggle to drive market share at first, although its marketing team have plenty of Big Pharma commercial and launch expertise, a Tonix presentation listing experience at Pharma giants Amgen (AMGN), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and biotechs Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM) and Elutia and Syneos Health.

Analysis & Concluding Thoughts - Is Tonix Stock A Buy, Sell Or Hold Post Approval?

I wrote in my last note on Tonix:

The August PDUFA data for TNX-102 can only be described as a critical moment for Tonix - succeed, and I suspect shares will spike on news of the first new fibromyalgia treatment in 15 years. Fail, and after four reverse stock splits in two years, I suspect Tonix shares would plunge in value, putting the company's future as a public entity in doubt.

Now we know that Tonix succeeded, can management succeed with a commercial launch?

This is now the critical question, and I actually feel more optimistic than back in May. The effort that goes into winning commercial approval for a drug is significant and should not be underestimated.

Recently, however, there has been a trend in biotech stock valuations falling post drug approval - Adaptimmune (ADAP), Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) and Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL) spring to mind, although there are many other, better examples, as the market begins to think about whether all of the effort was worth the hundreds of millions of dollars spent, and whether there is really a market out there?

The fact there have been no new drug approvals in fibromyalgia in 15 years is a double-edged sword, in my view, as it either means the sector is crying out for better therapies, or is adequately treated with off-label drugs, or that patients struggle to find therapies that work.

To justify a market cap valuation of >$500m, and reward shareholders buying today on the positive news, long-term, I think Tonix needs to bolster its cash position, up SG&A spending and try to secure reimbursement from health insurers.

The key determining factor may be whether patients feel the additional benefit of Tonmya - I believe Tonix will need to drive ~$50m - $75m of revenues in 2026 to keep the market happy and maintain its current valuation, and how many patients Tonix is able to reach on a limited budget?

Given I believe a $2m revenues per quarter company may struggle with that ramp up in expectations, I am sticking with my prior thesis and assigning shares a "Hold" rating, although if the stock price trades >$60 at COB on Monday, I'd be tempted to lock in gains - Tonix stock is now up and astonishing ~350% year-to-date.

The brave investor may hold and show faith in the company, but if analysts forecasts are right and it takes 7-8 years to achieve $800m in revenues, then by that time patents for Tonmya will be about to expire. Still, if the drug can earn $1.5bn in those 7-8 years, it may just make Tonix an interesting M&A target.

Shareholders are being confronted with mainly positive choices as a result of the approval, which is all that matters today.

data.title: Banco do Brasil Q2: Another Troubling Quarter
data.description:
I'm downgrading Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY) to underperform after the Brazilian state-owned bank posted a very weak quarter in 2Q25, following an atrocious 1Q25 and soft guidance in 4Q24.

The main negative factors that have impacted the bank's thesis, which has accumulated more than 26% declines in the last twelve months—and ~30% since the 1Q25 results in mid-May—are centered on persistent defaults in the agribusiness front. Although there has been no significant crop failure in recent years, the sharp drop in corn prices, the subpar performance of soy, and Brazilian producers' high leverage have led to a sharp increase in defaults and requests for judicial reorganization.

In fact, the 90+ days delinquency rate increased in all other segments, reflecting a sharp increase in the cost of credit, highlighting the speed and magnitude of the deterioration.

In addition, a new resolution requiring the recognition of operating revenues on a cash basis has helped to pressure Banco do Brasil to deliver a return on equity (ROE) that has fallen to its lowest level since 2016, placing it among the lowest of Brazil's major incumbent banks.

These events trigger a reduction in payout, which is a conservative measure to preserve capital in the face of weak results, making the income thesis increasingly less attractive.

As I warned since 4Q24 that the guidance already anticipated the weaknesses that Banco do Brasil would face going forward, driven mainly by the increase in provisions and pressure on credit costs, this scenario materialized faster than expected, and with the valuation still in line with the same levels as at the beginning of this year, even with more pessimistic forecasts for the future.

The Root of Banco do Brasil’s Declining ROE

Before diving into the most recent numbers, it is important to take a more critical look at the context of the evident deterioration in the quality of Banco do Brasil's assets, which has become more apparent over the last few quarters.

Banco do Brasil, being a state-controlled bank, holds more than 50% of the country's rural credit, which, on the one hand, gives it a competitive advantage in this very important sector of the Brazilian economy, but also exposes it to greater risk when problems arise compared to its incumbent peers.

The root of the problem was actually the pandemic. In 2020, Brazilian agribusiness had to resort heavily to credit to maintain production, causing high indebtedness in the sector. It turns out that indebtedness worsened over the years with poor harvests and difficulties in rolling over debts.

As a result of the pandemic, inflation in Brazil skyrocketed, and the bitter remedy is high interest rates. The Selic (local interest rate), persisting at extremely high levels—even today at 15%—further deteriorated producers' cash flow, eroding profits and causing a snowball effect.

The result was reflected in rural credit, which now costs more than 20% when adding the Selic rate and bank spreads, something unsustainable for rural producers. And in unsustainable scenarios, the result is high default rates.

Data shows that default rates in Brazil's rural sector reached 7.9% in 1Q25, a jump of 0.0pp vs. the same period last year, while default rates among companies in the sector jumped 26% between March 2024 and March 2025. Requests for judicial reorganization reached extremely high levels in 1Q25, jumping 21.5% YoY.

To make matters worse for Banco do Brasil, the bank uses crop pledges as collateral, which is less efficient in cases of judicial reorganization. With this ease of access to funds, producers with the ability to pay choose to enter into judicial reorganization, which ultimately makes it difficult to enforce collateral.

This scenario then leads to an increase in provisions. In 1Q25, the total cost of credit (Allowance for Loan Losses/Provision Expense for Doubtful Accounts) stood at R$10.2 billion, up 9.6% QoQ and 19% YoY. In Agribusiness alone, it was around R$5.2 billion—a jump of $2.2 billion versus 4Q24.

The increase in the cost of credit (PDD), which in this case is recorded as an expense in the bank's income statement, has recently tended to trigger a decline in profits and a weaker ROE—the latter being practically the most important indicator in the banking sector. In this case, Banco do Brasil's adjusted net income fell from R$9.1 billion in 4Q24 to $5.8 billion in 1Q25, reflecting a drop in ROE from 21.5% to 16.1% in the same period—a rather alarming decline of 5 pp.

Banco do Brasil 2Q25 Update

Moving past the problems plaguing Banco do Brasil, let's look at the most recent figures. To begin with, nothing structurally different occurred between 1Q25 and 2Q25 that could radically alter the outlook. But the problem is that the figures continued to deteriorate, arguably at an even faster pace than anticipated.

In 2Q25, Banco do Brasil reported net income of only R$3.8 billion, implying a quarterly decline of 48% and an annual decline of 60%. The explanation for this huge contraction is again the consecutive deterioration in credit quality and the consequent increase in provisions.

The cost of credit jumped a whopping 104% YoY and 57% QoQ, reaching R$15.9 billion. Delinquency (90+ days) reached 4.2%, up 0.3pp QoQ and 1.2pp YoY. All weak numbers indicate a deterioration in the overall scenario.

Even worse was ROE, falling to a miserable 8.2%—a drop of 7.5 pp QoQ and 12.8 pp YoY, the lowest level since 2016 and consolidating itself among the lowest among the large incumbent banks.

In addition, Banco do Brasil has been impacted by a new resolution in Brazil's tax code, which requires the bank to provision more rigorously and in advance, in addition to restricting the recognition of revenues in high-risk operations, which ultimately tends to put pressure on profits and ROE and increase the volatility of results.

The decline in profits also forced Banco do Brasil to take conservative measures regarding shareholder remuneration, reducing its payout from 45% to 30%. The expectation is that a gradual return to historical levels of 40%-45% will occur as results normalize.

Finally, if the results for 1Q25 were so weak, but so weak, that Banco do Brasil suspended part of its guidance in 2Q25, at least the bank revised downwards virtually all suspended lines:

The main negative highlights are the expectations that (1) adjusted net income will fall 39% YoY, (2) credit costs will be between R$53 billion and R$56 billion, above the H1 2025 level of R$26.1 billion—signaling continued pressure in the short term; (3) a decline in credit portfolio growth from the previous 5.5%-9.5% to 3%-6%. Perhaps the only positive point was that Banco do Brasil projects that H2 will have slightly higher profits than H1, and that 4Q25 will be closer to 1Q25.

On Valuations

When we look at valuations, there is a clear mismatch between BDORY's price and book value, indicating that the market already prices in some of the future risks greater than the current equity suggests.

If we consider that the recent ROE (at 8.2%) continues at levels close to this, the book value per share grows slowly (~2% per quarter) and, maintaining the current P/B of 0.69x, the projected price of the ADR would be R$3.85. If the market increases the discount to 0.6x, reflecting (rightly) the higher perception of risk, the price should theoretically fall to around R$3.35, showing that the current decline actually reflects expectations of future risk rather than immediate accounting deterioration.

Although BDORY is significantly discounted when compared to its private peers, this reflects very clear fundamental factors: low profitability, higher credit risk, and growing provisions. Therefore, a bearish thesis is based precisely on the possibility that the market still underestimates the future deterioration of ROE and BVPS, keeping the price under pressure or even increasing the discount.

Upside Risks and Final Remarks

It has been a flood of bad news for Banco do Brasil's shareholders lately. As if the bad news in Q1 2025 wasn't enough, the results for Q2 2025 brought even more deterioration and poor expectations for the rest of the year.

The upside of this—and the main risk to the short- to medium-term bearish thesis—is that, considering that the market tends to prefer bad news to uncertainty, there is a greater chance that risks may be better priced in. However, I still see little sense in Banco do Brasil continuing to trade at metrics in line with what it was trading at the beginning of the year, when the scenario was less pessimistic and the deterioration of assets was not yet so pronounced.

With the bleak outlook for 2025 and little hope for structural improvement in the Brazilian agribusiness sector in the short to medium term, I believe that Banco do Brasil is likely to decouple from its private incumbents and underperform, at least over the next six months.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IHF: Attractive U.S. Healthcare Valuations Amid Sector's Recovery
data.description:
The healthcare sector in the U.S., despite facing short-term headwinds, is driven by strong trends: demographic shifts and innovation.

Given the current environment, I will rate the iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (IHF) as a buy. The fund is concentrated on top large holdings, market leaders adding resiliency to the fund’s positioning (UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Elevance Health (ELV) and Cigna Group (CI)) and despite the turbulence that the market is seeing, I think the fund is fairly priced, and the recent drop in price makes it attractive.

Managed Care’s Resilient Core

The portfolio of IHF is concentrated in two different ways. It is concentrated on its top companies: the fund holds 63 companies and has a 72.17% weight on its top 10, mainly driven by the top 3 (the companies I mentioned in the introduction) as they represent 41.59% of the fund. The other one is its sectoral concentration, which makes sense given it is a sector-specific fund. You can see below that the fund’s focus is on managed health care and on health care services, with a secondary focus on facilities and technologies.

As a peer fund, I identified the SPDR® S&P® Health Care Services ETF (XHS), which is also in the FactSet Equity: U.S. Health Care Providers & Services Segment. This fund has the same objective as IHF but with a very different approach. It is a lot more diversified, 8.38% in the top 10 and focused on small and midcaps (70% of the fund vs. 28% for IHF). The sectoral focus is also different, as you can see below:

I like the focus on Managed Health Care of IHF, as it is a sector projected to recover through 2028 by McKinsey: Managed Service Providers should see an 8% increase in EBITDA driven by a 6% increase in enrollments. In short, both sales and margins are projected to increase, giving a positive outlook. Another factor that should drive the recovery is the adoption of AI, with 58% of health executives announcing that they are monitoring AI innovation to improve their services, already widely used for claims processing, underwriting, personalized treatment plans, fraud detection, and document extraction.

I like less the fund’s high concentration, with the top holding United Health facing regulatory scrutiny over its dominant position, which could hurt the fund if restrictions are put in place to foster the competition, less represented on the fund. The market is overly optimistic about UNH now, as Warren Buffett announced in its latest reporting having bought 5 Mn shares.

If the concentration represents a risk, maybe in this case it is the smart thing to do. The trend now is to control costs, as clients are complaining about high costs. The sector is pressured by costs, the way to deliver profit in this situation is to benefit from scale and innovation, healthcare giants have the best weapons to do so, if they can prove to be agile enough.

Demographic and Technological Waves

I mentioned it before, the two major trends driving healthcare growth now are demographic shifts and innovation. Let’s look deeper at these trends.

It will not be a surprise for anyone, the population of the U.S. is aging, and this means that the share of the population with strong healthcare needs will increase. To what extent, see below.

The home healthcare market is surging and projected to grow at a 10% CAGR until 2030 to reach a size of $750 Bn. This is of course driven by demographic shifts, but also by the need to find more cost-efficient options, and by innovation, remote care is becoming more efficient. For IHF, with strong weights in health care services and facilities (34.04% and 15.71%, near 50% of the fund), this is an important trend captured by companies such as CVS Health (CVS) and HCA Healthcare (HCA), two companies with strong telehealth and outpatient solutions.

Innovation, and especially A.I. is changing the healthcare sector, with 77% of health executives ranking AI among their top three investment priorities in the next 12 months. This is especially true for claims processing, underwriting, personalized treatment plans, fraud detection, and document extraction, as I mentioned above. The fund has a small 8% stake in healthcare technologies, directly aimed at the companies at the forefront of this innovation, for example Teladoc Health (TDOC). I would have appreciated a higher weight to this sector, but broader healthcare companies are also benefiting from it, less directly.

Financial Metrics

The expense ratio of the fund is reasonable for a sector-specific fund, 0.38%. It is also aligned with XHS at 0.35%, which I find a bit odd. I would expect the small-cap fund to be more expensive, but this is not the case.

The large-cap strategy of IHF is more appealing for investors, the fund’s AUM is $701.2 Mn, almost 10x more than XHS, its closest competitor. The fund’s liquidity is very high; the average daily trading volume of the fund over the last 30 days was $30.5 Mn, a high figure linked to the $100 Mn net inflow that the fund saw in the last month.

The advantage with a large cap fund is also the ability to pay dividend: the fund’s 30-day SEC yield stands at 1.37% and covers a 1.01% dividend yield, far above that of XHS: only 0.29%.

Turning to valuation metrics, this is very surprising: IHF is a lot less expensive than its small/mid cap peer. The P/B ratio is almost the same around 2, but the fund’s P/E ratio of 16.38 is way below XHS’s 20.70, highlighting the recovery potential of IHF.

Now to the performance, you can find it below:

As you can see, there is nothing to be excited about when looking at the historical performance, holding the fund for 5Y barely got you any return, and the 5% return over the last 10Y is depressing. This is due to the abysmal 1Y performance of -25.6%, which I actually think represents a buying opportunity. Over the last 15 days, the fund actually recovered 6% (graph below), driven by UNH +8% over the same period. For now, I still think there is a buy opportunity, but if the price continues going up too much, this will be a hold.

Risks

I mentioned it before with UNH’s example: the fund's high concentration is a risk: 40% on the top 3 assets is huge and significantly exposes the fund’s performance to them, introducing a very high level of company specific risk.

An additional risk factor is the increase of costs: 7 out of 10 respondents to this analysis said that if prices keep going up, they won't be able to pay anymore, and this puts pressure on margins. This is an issue as medical costs are projected to increase 8.5% in 2026, this is not a small increase.

Macroeconomics are also a risk, with potential rate cuts expected for the end of the year, if delayed, they will have an impact on valuations, as the P/E ratio above 16 indicates that the market is pricing future growth in current valuations, thus depending on interest rates. The ratio is not high in other industries, and the impact would be moderate, but still to take into account.

Regulation is also a risk, because the sensitive nature of the healthcare industry puts it under a lot of scrutiny from global regulators. A change in the regulatory environment, for example, changes in drug pricing or in insurance claims in the U.S., could change the business model of the fund’s holdings. It could also create unexpected compliance costs for global holdings.

Cybersecurity can also be a risk, because healthcare companies are working with sensitive personal data. You can find this specific risk debated here. A striking figure to realize the importance of cybersecurity is the following: in 2024, healthcare data breaches concerned 70% of the U.S. population. The average cost of a data breach is $10 Mn.

Conclusion

IHF is poised to benefit from the recovery of the healthcare industry thanks to its exposure to large-cap leaders. Valuations are currently attractive, and the recent 1Y price dip represents a buying opportunity.

The fund is aligned with megatrends: AI innovation and demographic shifts, but also faces significant risks, especially price dynamics that could cap the upside potential.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - August 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

During the last month, the U.S. administration has imposed new tariffs on a host of countries but also finalized some high-profile trade deals, especially with Japan, South Korea, and the European Union. In all three cases, the U.S. will impose a tariff of 15% while there would be no reciprocal tariff from the other side. Even though the devil is always in the details, it looks like a victory for the administration with three of the major trading partners. Also, it appears that the administration has moved from a base 10% tariff to a 15% tariff for most of the world. Also, the trade discussions with China are still ongoing, and the previous pause has been extended by another three months. Moreover, we are likely not going to see the kind of tensions we had back in April, as both sides seem to have understood the pitfalls. Obviously, the markets are liking what they are seeing. Also, the 2nd quarter earnings season has been going strong overall, with over 90% of companies in the S&P500 having reported already. The year-over-year “blended” earnings growth rate for the S&P 500 is 11.8% (so far), which is pretty good. The market is making new all-time highs almost on a regular basis.

Also, in the meantime, the July jobs report was dismal, and the previous two months were also revised downwards. That raised a lot of hope for the interest rate cut by the Fed in its next meeting. Obviously, further progress of the market and the broader economy will depend a great deal on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months. However, the Fed has offered no hint of a rate cut during its next meeting, slated for September 2025. We have been saying for some time that the Fed is already late in lowering the rates, and the longer it waits, the greater the harm it may cause to the broader economy, the job market, and especially to the housing market. Nonetheless, the probability of a rate cut of 25 basis points (in Sep. FOMC) has gone above 90%. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, CI, CTRA, FIX, HWM, MLI, PWR, and RL (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: AVGO (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (50 - 10) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes STZ, CDW, CI, OTIS, MOS, OVV, LLY, NUE, and AMH (leaving us 31 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes DKS and CINF, leaving us with 29 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. We remove CARR, RGLD, MSCI, WTRG, CTRA, and VOYA. After removing these six names, we are left with 23 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep BBVA, but remove IBN.

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep BK.

Finance – Inv. Banking and others: We will keep MS but remove V.

Computer Software: We will keep MSFT and INTU.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO, but remove TSM.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM.

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep PWR.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed three names, so we are finally left with 20 (23-3) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (BBVA), (BK), (INTU), (MSFT), (RL), (EME), (PWR), (APH), (MS), (CW), (HWM), (TRV), (MLI), (WPM), (FSV), (AVGO), (TS), (CTAS), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Jul. 2025):

(FIX), (BBVA), (MSFT), (RL), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (WPM), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (SAN), (RL), (PWR), (APH), (MS), (HWM), (GILD), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. However, this month, similar to past trends, six stocks were retained, and four stocks were replaced with new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After 8.8%, 12.5%, and 5.80% gains in the previous three months, the portfolio continued to excel this past month with gains of 8.56% and also topped both the S&P 500 and the benchmark, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG), by a wide margin.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from Jan. 2010 until Jul. 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 10 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

The portfolio had a very strong showing this month, gaining 8.56%, after having strong gains during the past three months. It was also ahead of the S&P500 by a wide margin. The S&P500 and our benchmark ETF, VIG, gained 2.86% and 1.16% in the same period, respectively. The star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems (FIX), Broadcom (AVGO), Amphenol (APH), and Banco Santander (SAN). We also had attractive returns from Gilead Sciences (GILD) and National Fuel Gas Co (NFG). However, Howmet Aerospace (HWM) had a slightly negative performance this past month after strong gains in the previous months. Many of these stocks, except Gilead, are repeating for the month of August. Also, SAN is being replaced with similar banking stock Banco Bilbao (BBVA).

This month, four stocks are being replaced with new ones, which is about average. The new entrants are Banco Bilbao (BBVA), Microsoft (MSFT), EMCOR Group (EME), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM). The portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially at the present time. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: 6 Stocks I'm Buying As Tariffs Weigh Down The Economy (Chart Storm!)
data.description:
Get ready for enough charts to make your eyes cross!

In last week's article, "How I've Build A Monster Passive Income Portfolio," I did not provide an update on the economy or markets, which means I now have two weeks' worth of charts to drop.

It's gonna be awesome.

Here are our topics de la semaine:

  • The tug-of-war between disinflationary housing and inflationary tariffs within the CPI.
  • Some charts showcasing the K-shaped economy, wherein the thriving affluent are taking up the slack from the surviving (or suffering) paycheck-to-paycheck class.
  • The stock market is K-shaped as well, bifurcated between the AI-related winners and almost everybody else.
  • Some comments on my buy list and dividend growth investing in this increasingly K-shaped world.

Onward!

Inflation: The Tug-Of-War Between Tariffs and Housing

There's a tug-of-war going on in the CPI right now.

On one hand, you've got housing/shelter, which has been in a solid disinflationary trend for the last few years. These lagged shelter CPI metrics will continue to decline as they catch up to flattish real-time housing inflation.

On the other hand, you've got the highest average effective tariff rate in a century, which a mountain of economic research (example: see here and here) suggests will trigger price increases, which in turn will fuel a temporary bump in inflation.

So, while the shelter component of the CPI is exerting downward pressure on the inflation index, tariffs are slowly but surely exerting upward pressure on it.

As I wrote in a recent article:

According to economists at Goldman Sachs (source), as of June, US importing businesses have absorbed about two-thirds of the tariff costs, while consumers have absorbed 22% of it via price increases and exporters have absorbed 14% via lower export prices.

However, as you can see above, the Goldman Sachs economists predict that by the final months of this year, two-thirds of tariff costs will be absorbed by consumers via price hikes.

If they are right, then the CPI should experience a roughly 50 basis point bump between now and the end of 2025.

(My own view is that it will take a year or more for businesses to pass through most of the tariff costs to consumers because of the financial fragility of most American consumers today, but I directionally agree with the Goldman Sachs assessment.)

This past week, we got some hard data that corroborates the Goldman Sachs data. Producer prices (paid by businesses) surged in July as tariffs have had sufficient time to begin rippling through the economy.

This represented the fastest spike in producer prices in three years.

Businesses appear to be passing through tariff costs to other businesses, even if they're being slow and deliberate about passing through the costs to final consumers.

But there are areas where businesses are already raising consumer prices due to the tariffs.

Quoting the article again:

Economists from Harvard and the University of San Andres have been tracking the pricing impact of the tariffs in real time and publishing their findings every two weeks. By looking at pricing data from four large US retailers, they have found noticeable, albeit gradual, price increases that have been triggered by the imposition of tariffs.

Domestic goods have also risen in price, partly because many domestically produced goods use imported raw materials or intermediate parts and partly because domestic goods producers have used tariff protections as cover to expand margins by raising prices.

The auto sector is another obvious area where tariff-induced inflation is coming - and, indeed, already happening.

Auto repair and maintenance and car parts and equipment are seeing prices spike at their sharpest levels in years.

YoY Car Maintenance/Repair (Blue) and YoY Car Parts and Equipment (Green):

Since auto parts are tariffed at the same rate as finished cars in most cases, it's unsurprising to see tariff costs flowing through to consumers faster for these essential goods and services than for big-ticket durable goods like cars.

Quoting the article one more time:

Auto experts have warned about coming price increases since early April.

Likewise, along with higher car and auto maintenance/repair/parts prices come higher car insurance premiums.

Car insurance aggregator Insurify estimates that car insurance premiums should increase between 5-10% across the country by the end of this year.

So far, we have only just seen the beginning of the total tariff-induced inflation to come.

Now, on the other end of the tug-of-war rope is a strong disinflationary trend in the oversupplied housing sector, which accounts for about 35% of headline CPI and over 40% of core CPI.

YoY Rent (Blue) and YoY Owners' Equivalent Rent (Green):

As previously stated, both of these shelter inflation metrics lag real-time changes in market rent rates by a year or more.

Given the fact that market rents have been flat for about two years and will likely remain flat for a while longer amid an oversupplied multifamily market and weak job growth, I expect this disinflationary trend in shelter CPI to continue for the foreseeable future.

Interestingly, if you substituted shelter CPI for real-time residential rent changes, the CPI would have been at or below the Fed's target rate for years now.

Every month, Jeremy Schwartz of WisdomTree puts together a chart showing what the CPI would be using real-time market rent data from private sector sources instead of the CPI's lagged shelter metrics. Real-time inflation has been at or lower than 2% for the past two years, and even after a slight uptick in June-July from tariffs, it remains below 2%.

These charts show that while real-time inflation is slowly rebounding, it still remains quite low. This is a major reason why most people aren't yet seeing or experiencing the inflation to come.

The CPI will eventually re-converge with real-time inflation, but it's difficult to say how long it will take to accomplish that. And it's difficult to say whether tariffs will cause another short-term divergence like the post-COVID inflationary surge did in 2021-2023.

All we can say is that for the foreseeable future, the shelter component will continue to exert downward pressure on the CPI.

Meanwhile, tariffs will continue to exert upward pressure on the CPI.

The K-Shaped Economy In 5 Charts

In last week's article, I briefly mentioned the K-shaped economy, and I've discussed this topic in many past articles.

This concept refers to the divergent directions that have been taken in recent times by the affluent (high-income, asset owners, high savings rate) and the paycheck-to-paycheck class (low- to moderate-income, low net worth, little to no monthly savings).

This divergence is necessary to understand why many consumers are hurting and falling behind on bills even as total consumption continues to power forward.

From 2021 through 2023, fueled by "stimmy" money and a post-COVID reopening, there was a broad-based advance in consumer spending, inclusive of the low- and middle-income groups.

But as you can see, since 2024, low- and middle-income consumption has only grown about as much as consumer prices have increased, meaning that real consumption has flattened for the majority of Americans.

Interestingly, the chart above indicates that nominal spending by the top quintile of income earners has flattened since the beginning of 2024, indicating that their real consumption is actually down.

Even so, it's rich Americans who have the greatest capacity to grow their real consumption.

While wage growth for the lowest quartile (bottom 25%) of income earners has now dropped to below pre-pandemic levels, wage growth for the highest quartile (highest 25%) of earners remains fixed at almost 5%, higher than any time except 2023 in decades.

Here's another breakout of wage growth by income group, zooming in on the last few years in order to capture the major shift from the post-COVID labor shortage years to today:

Perhaps the flattening of consumption by the rich over the last year or so (even as their wages continue growing) paradoxically helps to explain the impressive performance of the stock market. Instead of the incremental dollar going toward spending, maybe it is instead going into the capital markets and bidding up stock prices.

Here's a wrench to throw into the situation: Recent college graduates appear to be, by and large, joining the ranks of the paycheck-to-paycheck class rather than starting down the road to affluence.

The burgeoning advancement of artificial intelligence over the last few years has led businesses to dramatically cut the number of new college grads they hire. From the pre-pandemic years to the present environment, the biggest jump in unemployment has been seen among bachelor's degree holders. The second biggest jump comes from graduate degree holders.

Put simply, more and more knowledge-based work is now being done by AI tools, not people.

That has fueled a continuation in what has actually been a long-term trend in rising underemployment among recent college grads.

The bottom rung of the post-collegiate career ladder now appears to be too high for many graduates to reach, which means that the barista drawing a smiley face on your coffee cup is increasingly likely to have a bachelor's degree.

My personal opinion is that the age of encouraging almost every high schooler to go to college is probably over. The pendulum ought to (and, I think, will) swing back toward the legitimization and dignification of trade schools and working with one's hands.

The K-Shaped Stock Market

Just as the economy is K-shaped, so too is the stock market.

It would only be a slight oversimplification to say that AI-related stocks are thriving while everything else is surviving (at best) or languishing (at worst).

A big reason for this is that big tech companies are deploying their massive war chests of cash into the AI buildout, which has ripple effects outward to semiconductor stocks, data center developers and landlords, independent power producers, and so on.

Rather shockingly, AI companies are pumping about 130% of their EBITDA into capex, while non-AI companies are spending about 50% of EBITDA on capex.

Talk about K-shaped!

Everyone involved in AI seems to be winning right now. Whether you're the gold miner or the guy selling picks and shovels, you're winning. (At least for now.)

The top 10 stocks in the S&P 500 (SPY) (save, perhaps, the 10th largest holding, Berkshire Hathaway) all have various irons in the AI fire, some more than others.

At this point, these top 10 mega-cap giants account for about one-third of total SPY earnings and ~40% of the SPY's total market cap.

The top 20 stocks, for what it's worth, account for more than 50% of SPY's market cap.

At risk of belaboring the point, that means that the bottom 480 stocks in the index account for less than half of its total market cap.

The performance of major stock market indices has rarely been more divergent from that of the broader economy than it is today.

The following is a chart that illustrates this within the stock market.

The Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) is populated exclusively by AI-related companies, while the larger Nasdaq Index (QQQ) is mostly but not entirely technology and AI-related companies. Meanwhile, for scale, I show the heavily value-oriented Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which have gone precisely nowhere over the last year.

Again, talk about K-shaped!

Averages have become incredibly deceptive in both the economy and stock market. Within those averages are contained both the thriving affluent and the languishing paycheck-to-paycheck class, both the thriving AI-related stocks and the languishing value-oriented stocks, both the cash-rich mega-caps and the debt-burdened small caps.

My Dividend Growth Buy List Today

It's a sobering thought for a value-oriented dividend growth investor like me that, whatever happens in the stock market in the near term (whether big tech pulls back, goes higher, or stays rangebound), the trends fueling the K-shaped economy and market may very well endure.

As population growth declines, Boomers continue retiring from the workforce, AI swallows up entry-level jobs, and tariffs whittle away purchasing power, it seems likely that these K-shaped patterns aren't going away anytime soon.

For now, it's enough to give me pause and spur contemplation but not enough to make me alter my investment strategy.

There are still some value-oriented yet GARP-y dividend growers I find attractive and buyable.

My "buy list" includes six of them:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Tower (AMT) 3.4% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.4% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Iron Mountain (IRM) 3.4% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.3% Mid-Single-Digit

One's long-term success as a dividend growth investor depends a lot on how accurately one can project forward dividend growth. I try to be fairly conservative in this department.

If you aim to begin withdrawing income from your portfolio in 10 years, it would be better to buy a 4%-yielding stock growing at 5% per year than a 5%-yielding stock growing at 2.5% per year or a 6% yield that doesn't grow at all.

Many of my readers are withdrawers or soon-to-be withdrawers who are trying to maximize current income that hopefully grows fast enough to beat inflation. I don't blame you!

But I'm a DGIer in my 30s trying to optimize my portfolio for maximum income 10-plus years from now.

That's why, if I'm buying something just for the income, I like to get at least a 6.5% yield, preferably 7% or higher.

Whereas, if I'm buying a dividend growth stock, I want to reasonably project a 10-year yield-on-cost of at least 6.5% (preferably over 7%).

Of course, it's very hard to forecast a 10-year YoC accurately, but my assumption is that if I get even decently close, it should be a successful DGI investment. "Shoot for the moon and land among the stars," as the saying goes.

Most of these stocks were on my buy list last week, so see that article for my still-relevant comments on five of the six names.

The only newbie is Permian Resources, a pure-play Permian Basin oil and gas producer.

I've been very impressed by the smart capital allocation of PR's young gun co-CEOs Will Hickey and James Walter. This year, they've managed to repurchase $43 million of stock (at very low prices), make multiple bolt-on acquisitions in the Permian, and earn PR's first investment grade credit rating (BBB-) from Fitch, all while keeping debt low at 1.0x net debt to EBITDA.

To be fair, short of some geopolitical event that causes oil and gas prices to spike, PR lacks any obvious catalyst to send its share price higher. But as long as the price of oil stays more or less rangebound going forward, the company should have plenty of cash flow to continue the spree of smart capital allocation.

PR's soaring post-COVID rally may be over, but now it's time for the dividend growth story to begin.

data.title: QQQI: Double-Digit Yield With Big Tech Power
data.description:
The NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is one of my favorite ETFs to buy right now. Combining monthly dividends, big tech exposure, tax efficient returns, and an actively managed options strategy that returns high yields and also has upside potential. QQQI has done better than the S&P 500 and has kept up with the Nasdaq in a roaring bull market. If QQQI can deliver this level of return in a market where its strategy should be at a disadvantage, the alpha in a more volatile, range-bound environment is significant.

QQQI has not been around for long, and so it is difficult to see how it would perform in different market conditions. We have had 2 years in a row where the markets have returned more than 20%, with 2023 being 24.23% and 2024 being 23.31%. As of August 14th, SPY is up 9.5% YTD. These returns are out of the ordinary, and way above the S&P 500's historical average of about 11%. I am not saying that we are going to get a crash, or even saying that this trend cannot continue, but QQQI is one of the strategies I am using to protect myself against a potential slowdown towards the end of the year or a red year in 2026 or 2027. Either way, if QQQI can capture around 98% of QQQ's return with its covered call strategy, it should heavily outperform the underlying in a sideways market or a bear market, where a covered call strategy shines. For me, I see QQQI as a no brainer way to go long the Nasdaq in a time when there could be uncertainty and volatility in the market.

High Income ETFs

A high-income ETF is an exchange traded fund structured to produce substantial, regular payouts by holding an underlying stock or index and selling options against it, typically through a covered call strategy. These ETFs have gained massive popularity over the past years, with more and more options coming out monthly. This competition is great for us consumers, but not all high-income ETFs are built the same. There are a lot of really high-yielding ones, and even ones that pay weekly, but the reality is that a lot of these will greatly underperform their benchmarks. Investors can suffer huge NAV (net asset value) erosion and inconsistent and unstable dividends.

In my books, what makes a great high-income ETF is the following:

1. Generates sustainable and consistent monthly income

2. No NAV erosion

3. Be able to capture some upside in bull markets

*Bonus point: Tax efficiency

As you may have guessed, QQQI checks every box.

QQQI's Options Strategy

QQQI usually uses 2 strategies to generate its monthly income + some upside if the market runs.

Selling Covered calls: QQQI primarily sells out of the money [OTM], usually 1.5-4% away from the current price. These are typically 6-7 weeks from expiration.

AS QQQI is a dynamic, actively managed ETF, it has prediction models and will typically only sell calls on 70%-80% of the shares it owns to capture some upside. But as this is dynamic, we can see the fund selling calls on up to 95% of shares or even as low as 60% depending on market conditions.

Buying Calls: Buying long calls when volatility is compressed to participate in upside. When volatility is low (VXN below 12), covered call premiums become much lower, so for the fund to meet income demand, it has to sell calls on 95%-100% of its shares. Buying long calls at this point would be used as an insurance policy to make sure that if the stock runs while they are heavily covered, they can have some upside appreciation.

NAV Erosion

NAV erosion, or Net Asset Value erosion, is when the value of an investment fund’s shares gradually decreases over time, even if the market stays flat or goes up a little. This can happen if the fund gives out more money to investors than it earns from its investments and options strategy. Essentially, the fund is dipping into its own capital to make those payments, which shrinks the overall value of the fund’s assets. This has not happened with QQQI, and I don't think this should be a concern. With its dynamic options strategy and great management, I have full confidence that QQQI will not suffer NAV decay.

Tax Efficiency

The IRS has specific types of "contracts" that get special tax treatment. One of these contracts is index options, as the ones we use on QQQI. These are called Section 1256 contracts. These contracts allow our profits to be taxed 60% as long-term capital gains, and 40% as short-term capital gains. This is a major win as long-term capital gains are taxed at a way lower rate than short-term, and these rules apply even if you held the contract for 1 day.

Additionally, much of the payout capital returned so far has been labeled as return on capital [ROC], which is not taxed immediately, and instead lowers your cost basis and is only taxed when the shares are sold or your cost basis becomes 0.

Breaking down QQQI:

  • Inception: January 30, 2024
  • AUM: ~$3.7 billion
  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%
  • Underlying Index: Nasdaq-100 (NDX)
  • Distribution: Monthly, much of it classified as return of capital [ROC]
  • Issuer: NEOS Investments
  • Notable Recognition: Winner of the Best New Active ETF at the 2025 ETF.com awards

QQQI vs. QQQ - Total Returns

Over the past year, QQQI has returned 27.46% compared to 29.23% for QQQ - an exceptionally narrow gap for a covered call ETF. This level of upside capture is impressive, especially as we are in a raging bull market, where covered call strategies typically lag further behind due to the upside cap from option writing. Historically, these funds do better in choppy or sideways markets, yet QQQI has managed to stay even with its underlying index while still delivering a double-digit yield. This speaks to the effectiveness of its actively managed options strategy, which balances income generation with meaningful participation in market rallies. All of these wonderful gains have much less volatility than the underlying, with Beta being about 1 on QQQI.

QQQI vs SPY - Total Returns

Over the past year, QQQI has outpaced the S&P 500 by more than 5%, and it’s done so in the middle of a bull market, a scenario where covered call ETFs typically lag rather than lead.

Peers
